title: Built to Last
subtitle: Successful Habits of Visionary Companies
author: Jim Collins
description: A landmark six-year Stanford research project revealing the timeless principles that distinguish visionary companies from their competitors, from clock-building to BHAGs to cult-like cultures.
keyIdeas: Clock building over time telling: build an institution that can thrive without you
Preserve the core ideology while stimulating progress
Big Hairy Audacious Goals (BHAGs) as catalysts for progress
Cult-like cultures create alignment around core values
Try a lot of stuff and keep what works (evolutionary progress)
Home-grown management promotes continuity of core ideology
Good enough never is: continuous improvement as a way of life
The genius of the AND: embrace paradoxical dualities
keyTakeaways: Visionary companies focus on building an institution, not on a single product or leader
Core ideology (core values + purpose) is immutable; everything else can change
BHAGs should be clear, compelling, and require extraordinary effort to achieve
Cult-like cultures ensure consistent behavior aligned with core values
Evolutionary progress through experimentation beats strategic planning alone
Promoting from within preserves cultural continuity and leadership depth
Continuous improvement (kaizen) is embedded in visionary companies' DNA
The 'Tyranny of the OR' is replaced by the 'Genius of the AND'
Visionary companies are more profitable than the stock market by 15x since 1926
Visionary companies were not founded by charismatic visionary leaders
whoShouldRead: CEOs and senior executives building long-term organizations
Entrepreneurs wanting to create enduring rather than flashy companies
Business students studying organizational behavior and strategy
Leaders navigating culture and values in growing organizations
